1: vayitpalel Yonah el YHWH elohav mim'ei haddag: 'Then Jonah prayed to the LORD his God from the belly of the fish.' The prayer of ch2 is a psalm of thanksgiving for deliverance already received — it does not petition for rescue from the fish but celebrates a rescue that Jonah views as having already occurred (when he was saved from drowning). The structural irony: Jonah's prayer is more theologically coherent inside the fish than his behavior had been outside it. Mim'ei haddag (from the belly of the fish) — me'ei literally 'entrails/inward parts' — the most visceral term for the fish's interior, underscoring the extremity of the location.
2: qarati mitzara li el YHWH vaya'aneni: 'I called out to the LORD out of my distress, and he answered me.' The psalm follows the standard lament-to-praise structure: crisis (tzara, distress) → cry to YHWH → divine response → deliverance. Mibeten sheol shivati — 'out of the belly of Sheol I cried' — the fish's belly is identified as Sheol (the underworld/realm of the dead), not a literal description but the experiential intensity of near-death and hopelessness. This identification is the basis for Jesus's typological reading: 'three days and three nights in the heart of the earth' (Matt 12:40) corresponds to Jonah's Sheol experience.
3: vatashlicheni metzulah bilevav yamim: 'You cast me into the deep, into the heart of the seas.' Jonah addresses YHWH directly as the agent of his trial — a bold theological move. The verb is vatashlicheni (you cast me) — the same root as the sailors' hashlichuni and God's hetil: the whole narrative is structured around this 'casting' motif. Metzulah (the deep, H4688) and levav yamim (heart of the seas) are the language of chaotic waters — the domain of death from which only YHWH can rescue. Mishebarecha vegalecha avari — 'all your waves and billows passed over me' — using possessive: YHWH's waves, not random forces.
4: va'ani amarti nigzarti mineged eineicha: 'Then I said, I am driven away from your sight.' The word nigzarti (I am cut off, H1644) carries the weight of exile — the same root used in Lam 3:54 and Ps 88:5 for being 'cut off' from divine care. This is Jonah's lament at its deepest: not physical death but covenantal separation from YHWH's face. The pivot follows immediately: ach osif lehabit el hekhal qodshecha — 'yet I shall again look toward your holy temple' — the turn from despair to hope. The temple (heikhal) is the point of YHWH's accessible presence; looking toward it is the posture of prayer (1 Kgs 8:48; Dan 6:10).
5: afafuni mayim ad nefesh: 'The waters closed in over me to my very life.' Three images of overwhelming: waters (mayim) to the nefesh (life/throat); the deep (tehom) surrounded him; suf chavush lero'shi — 'weeds wrapped around my head.' Suf (H5488) is the same word as in yam suf (Red Sea/Sea of Reeds): the plant that grows in the water of the Exodus crossing has become the thing that entangles in the water of near-death. The Exodus lexicon is inverted: the sea of deliverance is the sea of drowning, until YHWH reverses it again.
6: leqitsvot harim yaradti: 'I went down to the roots of the mountains.' The cosmology of the verse: the mountains' roots reach into the watery deep; their foundations (qitsvot, extremities/bases) are the lowest point of the earth. Haeretz beveriacheha baadi leolam — 'the earth with its bars closed over me forever' — the bolts (beriachim) of the earth-gates, the prison-lock of death itself. The stunning reversal: vataal mishachat chayai YHWH elohai — 'yet you brought up my life from the pit, O LORD my God.' Mishachat (pit/corruption, H7845) — the Hebrew word for the grave and decay; YHWH brings up from the lowest point.
7: behitatef alai nafshi et YHWH zacharti: 'When my life was fainting away, I remembered the LORD.' The verb hitatef (H5848, to faint/languish — used also in Ps 142:4; 143:4 for the spirit overwhelming within) marks the crisis point from which the turn to YHWH comes. Vatavoqefillati eleicha el hekhal qodshecha — 'and my prayer came to you, into your holy temple.' The word vatavo (and it came) — the prayer as a person traveling to reach YHWH's temple. The direction of prayer reverses the direction of flight: Jonah had fled from YHWH's presence; his prayer now arrives at YHWH's dwelling.
8: meshamerim havelei shav chasdham yaazovu: 'Those who pay regard to worthless idols forsake their hope of steadfast love.' This theological aphorism interrupts the autobiographical narrative to make a universal point: havelei shav (worthless vanities — the term hevel is Ecclesiastes' key word for futility; shav = empty/false) are the idols that the pagan sailors of ch1 had been crying to. To cling to them is to abandon (yaazovu) one's chesed — the steadfast covenant love that YHWH gives and that connects his people to him. The sailors had begun to turn away from their havelei shav (v5 → v16); this verse is the theological reason why.
9: va'ani beqol todah ezbeach lach: 'But I with the voice of thanksgiving will sacrifice to you.' The emphatic pronoun va'ani (but I/and I) contrasts Jonah with the idol-venerators: he will sacrifice with the voice of thanksgiving. Asher nadarti ashalema — 'what I have vowed I will pay' — vow-fulfillment in the temple was the standard act of thanksgiving for answered prayer (Ps 116:14; Ps 22:25). The closing confession: yeshuah laYHWH — 'salvation belongs to the LORD.' Three words that summarize the theology of the entire book: the nations, the storm, the fish, the worm — all belong to YHWH and all serve his salvation purposes.
10: vayomer YHWH ladag vayaqe et Yonah el hayabasha: 'And the LORD spoke to the fish, and it vomited Jonah out on dry land.' The fish obeys YHWH's single command — vayomer YHWH (the LORD spoke) is the creator's speech-act authority. The verb vayaqe (vomited, H6958) is blunt and physical — not a gentle release but an expulsion. El hayabasha (onto the dry land) — the same phrase used in 1:9 ('I fear YHWH, who made the sea and the dry land'): the God who made the dry land now has it receive his prophet from the sea. The descent-motif (ch1: down to Joppa → down into the ship → down into the fish's belly/Sheol) is fully reversed: Jonah is back on dry land.
